_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Guilford County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Guilford County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Greensborough in 1808. Early structure was a log courthouse, eventually abandoned and demolished.
Survived the Civil War intact. Union cavalry under Kilpatrick occupied Greensboro in April 1865 but did not burn public buildings. Replaced as county needs outgrew the building.
Second Greensboro courthouse served until replaced by the 1918 building. No fire or war damage.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Guilford County is one of the best-preserved counties in North Carolina. The courthouse survived the Civil War without burning — Union forces occupied Greensboro but did not destroy public buildings. No significant courthouse fire has occurred. Records run continuously from the county's 1771 formation, making Guilford an anchor county for Piedmont genealogy.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Guilford County after 1771, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps in the paper trail here point to the ancestor's circumstances — poverty, illiteracy, or transience — rather than to courthouse destruction.
record_losses.source: Arnett, Ethel Stephens. Greensboro, North Carolina: The County Seat of Guilford. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1955.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Early deed books (1771-1790) show some wear but remain legible.
deeds.repository: Guilford County Register of Deeds, Greensboro; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — wills and estate records survive from formation
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps. Loose estate papers from the earliest decades are fragmentary but bound volumes are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Guilford County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Excellent — marriage bonds from county formation
marriages.gaps: Bonds are more complete than registers for the earliest decades. Register books begin approximately 1851.
marriages.repository: Guilford County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — County Court minutes from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest volumes (1770s). Superior Court minutes begin approximately 1807.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Guilford County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Greensboro city health department records may predate 1913.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: Guilford County was a center of Quaker settlement in the colonial and antebellum periods. New Garden Monthly Meeting, Centre Monthly Meeting, Deep River Monthly Meeting, and Springfield Monthly Meeting kept meticulous records of births, deaths, marriages, removals (transfers), and disciplinary proceedings. These records are among the most complete pre-civil-registration genealogical sources in North Carolina.
substitute_sources.repository: Friends Historical Collection, Hege Library, Guilford College, Greensboro; some microfilmed at NC State Archives and FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Extraordinarily valuable. Quaker monthly meeting records often provide birth dates, parents' names, marriage witnesses (who were usually family members), and reasons for removal — information not consistently available in civil records until the twentieth century. For Quaker families, these records are superior to courthouse records in completeness and detail.
substitute_sources.source: Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Volume I: North Carolina. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Guilford County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880), manufacturing schedules (1820–1880), and slave schedules (1850–1860) add economic detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families between land and court records. Manufacturing schedules are particularly valuable for Guilford County given its early industrial development.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Guilford County, NC.

substitute_sources.source_type: Cone Mills and textile company records
substitute_sources.description: Cone Mills Corporation (White Oak, Proximity, Revolution plants in Greensboro) maintained extensive payroll, employment, and housing records from the 1890s onward. These records document workers' names, dates of employment, wages, and sometimes family members.
substitute_sources.repository: Greensboro Historical Museum; some records at NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying textile worker families and tracking their employment and residence. Payroll records can establish dates of arrival in Guilford County when census records are ten years apart.
substitute_sources.source: Greensboro Historical Museum. Cone Mills Collection finding aid.

narrative_hooks: Guilford County's courthouse survived the Civil War and every decade since, which means {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records almost certainly still exist — this is one of the best-preserved paper trails in North Carolina.

If {ancestor_name} was Quaker, the monthly meeting records at Guilford College may contain more detail about {pronoun_possessive} family than anything the courthouse holds — births, marriages, removals, and the occasional disciplinary minute that reveals character the civil records cannot.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Guilford County reflects the era, not the family — Quaker records, church rolls, and census records are the likeliest substitutes.

The Cone Mills employment records at the Greensboro Historical Museum may document {ancestor_name}'s arrival in Guilford County more precisely than the census — payroll records give dates, not just the once-a-decade snapshot of the census enumerator.
source_references: Arnett, Ethel Stephens. Greensboro, North Carolina: The County Seat of Guilford. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1955.
Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Volume I: North Carolina. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Greensboro Historical Museum. Cone Mills Collection finding aid.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: North Carolina County Records. Raleigh, 1937–1942.
